#growth-pod.text:
*Root Cause: utm_source == NA spike*

The spike in *utm_source = NA* started after the "d1 retained user event implementation". *App version 4.61.0*
UTM params are received from the attribution SDK and saved correctly (e.g., utm_source, utm_campaign, utm_medium). After this, the deeplink goes through redirect() for navigation. Inside redirect(), we were again trying to read UTM params from the deeplink URL (e.g., <stage://har/hin/show?showId=123>). Since the URL only has navigation params and no UTM keys, it saved an empty value overwriting the correct UTM data that was already stored. This caused all UTM fields to report as "NA".

*fixed :* Removed the redundant UTM extraction from redirect() since UTM data is already saved by the attribution SDKs before the redirect happens.

#growth-pod.text: <@U09AK8X90Q0> <@U08HTBH8MFA> international android subscription paywall

*One idea for users who don't take trial, what do you folks think about this?* 

*Spin the Wheel + Lucky Draw: Why and How*
*The problem we're solving:* 60% of paywall viewers see it 2+ times. Their Conversion drops from 31.8% on the first view to 16.9% on the second. By the third view, it's 14%. These are users who came to Stage for a specific movie, hit the paywall, left, and came back. They're interested but not converting. The paywall looks identical every time. We're leaving them no new reason to act.

*The idea:* On the 3rd paywall action, we show a Spin the Wheel. The wheel has 4 prizes: home theatre, Stage merchandise, iPhone, 1-year free subscription. The user spins and wins a home theatre (it always lands there, the randomness is cosmetic).

Then we show: "You've won a chance to get a home theatre! Watch [Sanwari] in your new home theatre. Start your free trial and complete the movie to enter the lucky draw."
The movie shown is the one the user was acquired from. We always have this data.
The user takes the trial, watches the movie (90%+ completion required), and gets a confirmed lucky draw entry. Watch more movies, get more entries. Progressive stacking: 1 movie = 1 entry, 2 = bonus entry, 3 = guaranteed small prize + entry.

*"Why would we give away a home theatre?"* Think about how FMCG works. Cadbury puts a scratch card inside a chocolate bar. "Win a trip to Malaysia." You buy the chocolate first. Then you scratch. Then you find out if you won. The chocolate company isn't giving away trips to Malaysia to everyone. *They're giving away the _chance_.* The cost of one trip to Malaysia is negligible against the incremental chocolate sales it drives.

Same logic here. The user takes a free trial first, then watches content, then enters a lucky draw. We're not giving away a home theatre to every user. We're giving the _chance_ to win one. The cost of one home theatre per month/quarter is negligible against the incremental trial activations and content consumption it drives.

*Activation angle:* This isn't just an acquisition play. Leaderboard. "Top 10 viewers this month get 5x lucky draw entries." Watch more, climb the leaderboard, increase your chances. This creates a competitive loop that drives consumption beyond the initial movie

*Any thoughts is it worth trying or any other ideas we can think around for those 70% users who don't take trial? <!channel>*

:bar_chart: 1-Day vs 7-Day Trial Plan Analysis

We did a preliminary analysis comparing our 1-day trial plan (`trial-exp-nov-1-199`) vs the 7-day trial plan (`trial-monthly-07-199-aug2025`). Key findings:

1. Trial Rate (TR)
• 7-day plan: 26.70% | 1-day plan: 25.90%
• 1-day plan has slightly lower TR (~3% relative drop), which is expected — shorter trial feels less compelling at first glance
2. Subscription Rate (SR) — where it flips
• D0 SR (within completion window): 1-day = 34.10% vs 7-day = 31.10%
• The 1-day plan converts trial users to subscribers at a higher rate — users who commit to a shorter trial are more intent-driven
3. Net New Subscribers (per 100 paywall views)
• :trophy: 1-day plan: 8.83% vs 7-day plan: 8.30% → 6.4% relatively higher for 1-day
• Despite lower TR, the higher SR more than compensates
4. Renewal Rate (RR1) — the strongest signal
• D60 Renewal: 1-day = 53.10% vs 7-day = 44.10% → 20% relative lift
• Users acquired via 1-day trial renew significantly more — these are higher-quality subscribers
5. Renewal Cancellation (RCR1)
• 1-day: 17.7% vs 7-day: 16.4%
• Cancellation after first renewal is nearly the same — so the higher renewal isn't coming from low-intent users who churn right after
Bottom Line:
1-day trial brings slightly fewer trials but significantly better downstream economics — more subscribers per paywall view, and those subscribers renew 20% more with no meaningful increase in post-renewal cancellation. The 1-day plan produces higher LTV cohorts.

#retention.text:
Hi <@U0A3B4GLZRS>,
From tomorrow onwards, we will be using *AppsFlyer as the primary source for daily reporting and campaign performance tracking* for *ReEngagement Campaigns* instead of Amplitude.
The reason is simple: our campaign objectives are *App Opened* and *Consumption Started*, and both these events are flowing and attributed correctly within AppsFlyer. Since AppsFlyer is the attribution platform, it reflects the numbers aligned with platform optimization and billing logic.
For example: While analyzing the *M1 Watcher cohort*, we observed a significant discrepancy between Amplitude and AppsFlyer data. The user counts and event volumes do not match, which is leading to inconsistent performance evaluation and decision-making challenges.
To avoid reporting confusion and ensure alignment with campaign optimization signals, we will now rely on *AppsFlyer numbers for all performance tracking, daily reporting, and analysis.*
*<@U07LFSB0PM5>* *<@U089APN985P>*

root.text:
:dart::dart::dart: *Continue Watching* notification test results:

*Phase 1 Timeline -* Early Jan 2026
*Experiment Setup -* Test 3 groups:
1. Send CW notif. after 1 hour of no watch event _no further reminders were shared_
2. Send CW notif. after 1 day of watch inactivity; Send top 3 most recent items (watched in the last 7 days) on 3 consecutive days or till the user watches anything, whichever comes earlier
3. Send CW notif. after 1 day of watch inactivity; Send top 3 most engaged items (watched in the last 7 days) on 3 consecutive days or till the user watches anything, whichever comes earlier
Group A worked the best across all metrics: CTR, Day on Day Watch Retention

*Phase 2 Timeline -* 26-Jan - Ongoing
*Experiment Setup -* Send CW notif. after 1 hour of no watch event, send reminders after 1 hours / 1 day / 2 day / 3 day in case of no watch activity; rotate top 3 latest titles on subsequent days of inactivity

*** *Results on scaled data ****
Clickers showed massive lift (2x retention), but only 0.8% of users clicked thereby limiting overall platform impact

:bar_chart: ***What worked:***
• Clickers / Users Who Engaged with the CW Notif. : *20%* D1 retention vs *11%* control (91% lift)
• Gains held through D3 (*13% vs 5%*) and D7 (*8% vs 3%*)
• *+9mins* delta in watch time per day for clickers
• Resume rate up of the title sent to *9.4%* from *7.8%* baseline

:chart_with_downwards_trend: ***The gap:***
• Only 0.8% of users clicked on the notification; while higher compared to other notifications - can be pushed further
• Non-clickers performed identical to control
• Blended impact gets washed out by the 99%+ who ignored the notification

***Bottom line:*** The notification works really well for the right users. We need to figure out how to get it in front of more of them — ensuring most relevant notifications do not get buried in notification tray.
Additionally, there is a case to be made that clickers by default may show better numbers than non-clickers, but I think that should be tested based on the suggestion mentioned below.

<@U07LFSB0PM5> - I strongly believe we should keep a pool of 10%-20% users and target them with intentional / relevant content only just to benchmark their performance against those who get all. E.g. CW CTR itself can be easily pushed to 1%-2% if we can get this notification more eyeballs.

PS - Screenshots of numbers (as of today) are attached below.

#tech-mates.text:
*COST OPTIMIZATION UPDATE* :rocket:
 *Implemented solution to reduce NAT Gateway data transfer costs*

  Our aws resources  running in private subnets (EKS clusters) need to access S3 buckets (media files, backups,
  configs). Until now, this traffic was taking the expensive path:

  Pod → NAT Gateway ($0.045/GB) → Internet Gateway → Public Internet → S3

  Even though both our VPC and S3 are physically inside the same AWS region (ap-south-1), every S3 request was exiting
  through NAT Gateway, getting charged per GB, then routing back into AWS to reach S3. We were essentially paying to go
   out and come right back in.

  This was costing us ~$1,500+/month in NAT data processing alone, and growing — 18% increase from Dec to Jan.

  Solution: S3 VPC Gateway Endpoints

  Deployed S3 Gateway Endpoints on both Prod and Non-Prod VPCs. These add a route table entry that directs S3 traffic
  through AWS's private backbone directly:

  Pod → Gateway Endpoint → S3 (private AWS backbone, $0)

  No NAT, no public internet, no cost.

  Before Implementation (Prod VPC):
  - NAT processing ~25,000-34,000 GB/month
  - Avg data through NAT: ~5,100 MB every 5 min
  - S3 burst spikes of 2-7 GB hitting NAT every few minutes
  - Monthly NAT data cost: ~$1,500-$1,900

  After Implementation (Prod VPC — observed within 30 min):
  - AZ1 NAT traffic dropped 86% immediately
  - S3 burst spikes — completely eliminated
  - Avg data through NAT: ~3,600 MB every 5 min (and still settling)
  - Estimated Expected  monthly savings: ~$570-$900/month (~$7K-$11K/year) ,

  Zero downtime. . Zero cost — S3 Gateway Endpoints are free,  cc <@U039SEJGKNZ> <@U09LM6696F2>  <!subteam^S06CC4EMSH2>.   .

Hi <!channel>,

As the financial year is nearing completion, please note the following reimbursement submission timelines:
• All reimbursements pertaining to *FY 2025–26* must be submitted *on or before 10th January 2026*. Any claims related to FY 2025–26 submitted after this date will *not be reimbursed*.
• Reimbursements for expenses incurred between *11th January to February 2026* must be submitted *on the paid date or latest by 2nd March 2026*.
• Reimbursements for expenses incurred in *March 2026* must be submitted *on the paid date or latest by 2nd April 2026*.
No reimbursements shall be processed after the above-mentioned deadlines, and any such expenses will have to be borne solely by yourself.
Requesting everyone to adhere strictly to these timelines for a smooth year-end closure.
CC: <@U05UY430AEQ>

#stage-ke-krantikaari.text:
*`MOST IMPORTANT READ FOR EVERYONE`* <!channel>

`Imagine Your Personal WhatsApp Chat`
You assume it is private because:
• It is password-protected
• It is accessible only to selected people
• It is not publicly searchable
*Now imagine:*
• Anyone with a link could read it
• It appeared in Google search
• It was visible without authentication
Would you still call it “personal”?
Of course not.
*The same logic applies to STAGE systems.*
If a staging website, payment workflow, internal PPT, or analytics dashboard is publicly accessible —
 even unintentionally — it is no longer internal.

:mag_right: `Why This Is a STAGE Business Responsibility`
In the context of STAGE , this includes:
• Payment systems & subscription financial workflows
• Sensitive internal PPTs, business strategy decks & growth plans
• Pre-launch features, experiments & product releases
• User data environments & analytics dashboards
These are *core OTT business assets*, not just tech deployments.
*If publicly accessible →*  Google can crawl, index, and cache automatically.
*Risk =*  Data Exposure | Revenue Impact | Competitive Leakage | Compliance Issues | Brand Trust Damage

:globe_with_meridians: `How Google Fetches Information`
• Automatically crawls publicly accessible links
• Follows discovered URLs
• Indexes content unless explicitly restricted
• Stores cached versions
If it is public and not blocked — it can be indexed.

:shield: `STAGE Mandatory Protection Framework`
• Password Protection (Hard Lock)
All staging & internal environments must require server-level authentication.
• No-Index Configuration
Apply global *noindex, nofollow, noarchive* rules.
• Robots.txt Blocking
Block all crawlers from accessing staging environments.

:white_check_mark: `Outcome`
Zero Indexing. Zero Caching. Zero Accidental Visibility.

:vertical_traffic_light: `Non-Negotiable Rule`
No staging asset goes public without:
• Business sign-off
• Security validation
• Explicit indexing approval
:pushpin: `Master Protection Prompt`
> Secure all STAGE staging and internal environments by enforcing server-level password protection, global no-index/no-follow headers, and robots.txt crawler blocking to guarantee zero public or search engine visibility.
